description: If something exists, then something exists — no matter what you call it.
goal: (-> (exists x (App P x)) (exists y (App P y)))

guides.text: The goal is $\exists x.\; P(x) \to \exists y.\; P(y)$. This should be trivially true — if something is $P$, then something is $P$, regardless of the variable name. But to prove it, we need `exists-elim`: from the existential claim, we open a witness, prove the conclusion under that witness, then discharge.
Assume the existential claim: something is $P$.
Introduce a fresh witness $y$ with `fix`. This is the arbitrary term we'll work with — 'let $y$ be some thing.'
Assume the witness satisfies $P$: $P(y)$. This is the instantiated body of the existential, under the fresh name $y$.
Merge the witness atom $y$ with the assumption $P(y)$ using `and-intro` so both are in the same context.
Extract $P(y)$ from the merged context. Now you have $P(y)$ at a step whose context contains both $y$ and $P(y)$.
From $P(y)$, form $\exists y.\; P(y)$ using `exists-intro`. Replace the concrete term $y$ with the binding variable $y$ — same name, but now it's the bound variable of the quantifier.

Now `exists-elim` discharges everything. It takes the existential (step 1), the witness subproof (step 6), and the witness variable name ($y$). The engine checks that $y$ doesn't leak into the conclusion and removes the witness atom and its instantiated body from the context.
Step 7 gives us $\exists y.\; P(y)$ under the assumption $\exists x.\; P(x)$. Discharge to finish.

unlocks.name: ∃-elim
unlocks.description: From an existential claim, open a witness $y$, prove the conclusion $\psi$ under the assumption $\varphi[y/x]$, and discharge — provided $y$ doesn't escape into $\Delta$ or $\psi$.
